text:
Visited the Colosseum last month with my brother, and it was truly an unforgettable experience. Seeing such an iconic piece of history in person is something else entirely. No matter how many pictures or videos you’ve seen, nothing compares to standing inside and imagining what it must have been like centuries ago.

We also explored Palatine Hill and the surrounding attractions, and I’m glad we took the time to see everything. Walking through the ruins and learning about the history made the entire visit even more meaningful.

The area is well organized, and there’s so much to take in. If you’re visiting Rome, this is an absolute must-see. I’m really glad I got to experience it with my brother — definitely one of the highlights of our trip to Italy.

Colosseum is definitely one of the most impressive historical places in Rome and absolutely worth visiting at least once. Seeing it in real life after watching it in movies and documentaries feels completely different. The size, architecture, and history are incredible.

A few things that are very important to know before going:

- Full Experience tickets usually become available around 1 month before the visit date.
- Another Full Experience option without the Underground area is usually released about 7 days before.

It’s much better to buy tickets at those times directly, otherwise you may end up paying several times more through reseller websites or tour companies.

For entry, arriving about 15 minutes earlier is enough. In our case it was organized and not as chaotic as expected.

Also, having an ID card or passport photo on your phone was enough for us.

At night the Colosseum becomes illuminated and looks really beautiful from outside too, so even walking around it in the evening is a great experience.

Inside, the atmosphere is very different from seeing photos online. You can really feel the scale of Ancient Rome when standing there.

An unforgettable experience under the stars!
We took the night tour of the Colosseum, and it was absolutely magical. Seeing this iconic landmark illuminated against the dark sky is a completely different experience from visiting during the day. The dramatic lighting highlights the architecture and creates an incredibly powerful atmosphere. It felt like stepping back in time to Ancient Rome. Plus, it was much cooler and way less crowded. If you want a unique perspective on history, the night tour is a must

The Colosseum is one of those rare places that somehow survives both history and tourism. Yes, it is crowded. Yes, there are queues, selfie sticks, and the occasional overheated pilgrim regretting sandals. And yet, the moment you step inside, the noise softens into something strangely reflective. Two thousand years later, the old arena still knows how to hold an audience.

Walking onto the arena floor is the highlight for many, but the underground hypogeum is where the imagination properly wakes up. Beneath the arches and worn stone lies the machinery of spectacle — cages, tunnels, lifts, and all the practical engineering behind imperial entertainment. A guided tour is genuinely worth considering, not only for the historical context but also for the blessed possibility of avoiding the longest queues.

The scale remains astonishing. Even visitors who find parts of the interior a little sparse tend to admit the atmosphere is unforgettable. There is something oddly philosophical about standing in a monument built for mass amusement while quietly contemplating civilisation, vanity, and mortality under the Roman sun.

A practical note: book only through the official Colosseo website. Rome may have perfected architecture, but modern ticket scams are flourishing nicely too.

Go early morning or near sunset if possible. The light becomes softer, the crowds thinner, and the whole place feels less like a tourist attraction and more like a surviving memory.

A bucket list place to visit. The only 1 of the 7 wonders of the world, therefore 1 that is easier to get to for people from the U.K and Europe. I wasn't disappointed one bit. I paid extra to have a guide and have a tour of the underground part of the amphitheatre, although not cheap for this option, I would definitely recommend as it was good to get fed lots of information and to be able to ask questions.

Also good to visit on an evening, so you can get stunning photos of it lit up.

The crowd's there for a reason. Look and behold! If you have not purchased the tickets, queue and make friends while you are at it. Souvenir shop at the end of the tour is a chance to grab some memorables of this iconic site.

The Colosseum is one of the most famous places in Rome and you have to see it if you visit the city. I was staying about 20 minutes walking from it, so I passed by the area more than once. Seeing it up close was a really nice experience.

Buy your ticket in advance because the lines are very long. You will wait a lot and walk a lot, especially in the sun. The ticket costs around 18 euros and it includes access to the Colosseum and the Roman Forum.

The Roman Forum was actually the best part for me. It is big, open, and full of ancient ruins. You can really feel the history there, but be ready to walk a lot.

Inside the Colosseum was okay, but in my opinion it is not necessary. Seeing it from outside is already very impressive. Overall, it is a must visit in Rome, just plan well and wear comfortable shoes.

An amazing experience — but I highly recommend reading about the place, watching a short video, or even asking an AI about its history before visiting. It makes the whole experience much more meaningful and impressive. I truly recommend it to everyone. Also, make sure to come back at night and admire it from the outside — it looks absolutely stunning.

We visited the Colosseum in Rome, and it was truly an amazing experience. The place was extremely crowded, and the lines were very long, but it was all worth it.
We got to learn so much about the history of the Colosseum and the Roman Empire. Seeing such a historic and iconic place in real life felt incredible. The architecture and atmosphere were absolutely breathtaking.
There was a lot of walking involved, and by the end we were quite tired, but overall it was a wonderful and memorable experience
Cette œuvre d’art architectural construit en huit ans est magnifique. Quand vous êtes dedans, vous pensez à  ces combats, à ces animaux, aux spectateurs. C’est un édifice majestueux.

Booked a TripAdvisor tour with City Walkers Tours. Our tour guide was Sam! Despite the draining heat wave, our tour guide, Sam, kept the group engaged with his lively energy. He did a fantastic job of weaving historical facts into relatable, everyday stories, making the content both fascinating and easy to digest. This was my second time taking this tour, and I highly recommend it—Sam’s depth of knowledge is truly impressive!

Visiting the Colosseum was an unforgettable experience and a true highlight of our trip.
Seeing such an iconic and historic landmark up close is incredible. The sheer scale of the structure and the history behind it are awe-inspiring. Walking through and imagining the events that once took place there really brings ancient Rome to life.
If you can, visit both during the day and at night. The atmosphere completely changes — during the day you can fully appreciate the detail and structure, while at night it’s beautifully lit and feels even more dramatic and atmospheric.
It can be busy, so allow plenty of time, but it is absolutely worth it. A must-see when in Rome.

The full experience at the arena was certainly worthwhile. I highly recommend booking tickets in advance. I personally had to wake up at 3 AM Eastern Time to secure tickets as they were released, and I believe they were released in 30-minute intervals. I encountered several instances where tickets were sold out by the time I logged in.

Went for the attic full experience and this is what I would recommend you do. It’s not crowded at all and we felt as if the upper areas we had access to was very private and you could take in the views of the colosseum as well as Rome from a Birds Eye view! After you also get to walk to see the arena and underground from the ground level which was great.

The Colosseum is absolutely magnificent — the greatest ancient monument I’ve ever seen! Standing before it, you can’t help but be amazed by its massive size, incredible architecture, and the unbelievable history it holds. Every stone tells a story of gladiators, emperors, and ancient Rome. Walking inside, seeing the arches, the arena floor, and the underground tunnels is breathtaking. It’s huge, powerful, and feels almost magical. Even after thousands of years, it still stands tall and proud, a true masterpiece of engineering. No words can describe how special it is — a world wonder that every person must visit at least once. Truly unforgettable!

I mean, it is the Colosseum. Who am I to criticize? It is one of the most famous landmarks in the world for a reason. No matter how many photos you have seen before, it is still impressive when you stand in front of it. The scale, the history, and the fact that it has been standing here for nearly 2,000 years make it a must-see when visiting Rome

It was a perfect experience. We bought a ticket to the Colosseum for a specific day and time in advance. We arrived about 5 minutes before and entered the Colosseum practically without any problems. In general, the line went much smoother than the experience at the Vatican Museums. Have your passport or ID ready. When entering the Colosseum, they check your ID and tickets (an electronic QR on your mobile is enough). Then there is a security check - similar to at the airport. But you can have drinks ;-). At first glance, entering the Roman Forum seemed a bit long. The line seemed to be at least an hour long if not more, when entering they also check your ticket and then there is a security check - it is slow because there is only 1 check. The positive thing is that there are more entrances/exits to the Roman Forum, so you have to choose. We were scared off by the heavy downpour, so we returned around 9:00 the next morning (the ticket is valid for 24 hours, which is really great and suited us) and we passed through the entrance without waiting, and there weren't that many people inside the complex.

Quite an extraordinary place to visit. The grand structure is truly awe-inspiring, and imagining how gladiators fought here thousands of years ago gives you a special feeling.

I’d definitely recommend taking a guide, as it makes the experience even more meaningful. Guides share fascinating facts and stories you wouldn’t usually come across on your own.

Also, make sure to buy tickets in advance, especially during the high season, as the Colosseum gets very crowded.

An amazing experience overall.

I hadn’t originally planned to go inside the Colosseum, but I’m so glad I did — it ended up being one of the most interesting and memorable experiences of my trip.

I explored on my own and listened to a podcast on Spotify called “Dark Side of Ancient Rome: Colosseum,” which was both fascinating and humorous at times. It really brought the history to life and made walking around even more engaging.

I purchased a disabled access ticket as I wear hearing aids. I did experience a bit of difficulty at the entrance, as staff weren’t initially clear on my disability. I brought proof of my hearing records and my Freedom Pass, although there was some confusion because it doesn’t specify the nature of my disability (which, to my understanding, isn’t required to be stated). After a bit of back and forth, everything was resolved, and I was able to enter.

I spent around an hour inside and thoroughly enjoyed it. There are shaded areas as well as sections in direct sunlight, so it’s worth keeping that in mind if you’re visiting during the summer. Although it was crowded and movement can be slightly staggered at times, it didn’t take away from the experience.

The gift shops inside were extremely busy with long queues, so I chose not to visit them. Overall, despite the minor entry issue and the crowds, it was a fantastic experience and absolutely worth going inside.

We booked an educational tour through the colosseum website. Highly recommend!! We were encouraged to spend time after our tour going back for photos, and after an hour of education and stories we were turned loose to explore on our own. It was an hour long, completely worth the price.

Once thing I didn’t realize is you can get down basically to the arena level with the regular ticket, so we didn’t need to purchase arena floor tickets separately, but the view from the arena platform was spectacular, and we’re glad we did.

Pro tip: it wasn’t super busy on the back side of the colosseum between the arena floor access point and the group access point, plenty of opportunities for photos of the Colosseum without people in them!
Took the tour and the history was amazing.  Went to the underground level and couldn't believe how cramped it all was.  Definitely something to see and learn about.

The Colosseum is very Busy all the time. I would highly recommend buying tickets weeks or even months in advance, especially for the full experience. Pack water, snacks and Euros to use the restroom. Once inside, the bathroom is free in most places.

The Colosseum is very large and easy to find. It's beautiful in person. The history inside is breathtaking and id highly recommend anyone to go and experience both inside and out. They do have 2 gift shops and a few vending machines inside too. There is no time limit for how long you can be inside ether. There are lots of picture opportunities everywhere too.. Definitely a one in a lifetime opportunity to have on your bucket list.

Visiting the Colosseum was an absolutely unforgettable experience and one of the most impressive historical landmarks I have ever seen.
Standing in front of this massive ancient structure immediately makes you appreciate the incredible engineering and architectural brilliance of the Roman Empire. The scale, history, and preservation of the Colosseum are simply breathtaking.
Walking through the interior and imagining the gladiator battles and events that once took place there creates a powerful connection to ancient history. Every corridor and arch feels filled with centuries of stories and culture.
What impressed me most was how majestic the Colosseum looks both during the day and illuminated at night, when the atmosphere becomes even more magical and dramatic.
Overall, a must visit destination in Rome historic, iconic, and absolutely extraordinary. Mamma mia, che meraviglia! 🤌

الكولوسيوم في روما يُعد من أعظم المعالم التاريخية في العالم، وهو رمز واضح لقوة الحضارة الرومانية القديمة وعظمتها الهندسية. بُني هذا المدرج الضخم في القرن الأول الميلادي في عهد الإمبراطور فسبازيان، ثم اكتمل في عهد ابنه تيتوس، وكان الغرض منه إقامة العروض العامة التي تجذب الناس من مختلف طبقات المجتمع. ومع مرور الزمن أصبح الكولوسيوم واحدًا من أشهر الآثار التي تمثل تاريخ روما القديم، بل ومن أشهر المباني التاريخية على مستوى العالم كله.
يمتاز الكولوسيوم بتصميمه المعماري المذهل، فقد شُيّد على هيئة مدرج بيضاوي ضخم يتسع لعشرات الآلاف من المتفرجين. هذا الاتساع الكبير لم يكن مجرد استعراض للقوة، بل كان دليلاً على التقدم الهندسي للرومان في ذلك العصر. فقد صُممت مداخله ومخارجه بطريقة دقيقة تسمح بدخول الجماهير وخروجهم بسهولة، كما استُخدمت فيه الأقواس الحجرية والطبقات المتدرجة التي أعطته شكله الشهير وهيبته المعروفة. وحتى اليوم، لا يزال الزائر يشعر بالدهشة حين يرى ضخامة البناء وبقاياه الصامدة رغم مرور قرون طويلة.
ولم تكن أهمية الكولوسيوم في حجمه فقط، بل في مكانته التاريخية أيضًا. فقد كان مركزًا مهمًا للحياة العامة في روما، وشهد أنواعًا متعددة من العروض التي كانت تعكس ثقافة ذلك العصر واهتماماته. ولهذا السبب يعد دراسة حية للتاريخ، لأنه يكشف للناس كيف عاش الرومان، وكيف كانت تُنظم مناسباتهم العامة، وكيف استطاعوا بناء منشأة بهذه العظمة بإمكانات ذلك الزمن. كما أن بقاءه إلى اليوم، رغم الزلازل والعوامل الطبيعية وأعمال التخريب التي مر بها، يبرز مدى قوة بنائه وروعة تخطيطه.
ومن أكثر ما يميز الكولوسيوم أنه ليس مجرد مبنى حجري قديم، بل شاهد على زمن كامل من الحضارة والسياسة والفن والهندسة. عندما يقف الزائر أمامه، فإنه لا يرى جدرانًا وأقواسًا فقط، بل يرى أثرًا من آثار أمة كانت من أقوى أمم العالم القديم. ولهذا ينجذب إليه ملايين السياح سنويًا، لأنه يمنحهم فرصة نادرة لرؤية التاريخ ماثلًا أمامهم بصورة واضحة وملموسة.
كما أن موقع الكولوسيوم داخل مدينة روما يزيد من قيمته، لأنه يقع في مدينة مليئة بالآثار والمتاحف والمعالم القديمة، مما يجعل زيارته جزءًا من تجربة تاريخية متكاملة. ويمكن للإنسان أن يتأمل فيه عظمة العمارة القديمة، ويتعرف من خلاله على جانب مهم من تاريخ أوروبا والعالم القديم. لذلك يُعد الكولوسيوم أكثر من مجرد معلم سياحي مشهور؛ إنه صفحة مفتوحة من التاريخ، ومثال خالد على براعة الإنسان في البناء والتخطيط، ورمز عالمي للحضارة الرومانية التي ما زال أثرها حاضرًا في العالم حتى اليوم.

This was on my list as item number one to check out while in Rome.  What is amazing is it looks like the prototype for any modern sports arena in the world. The museum is cool as it shows how they used rope and pulleys to get the animals up onto the main level for the gladiators to fight. Its pretty cool to see history like this up close. If in Rome, you have to take some time out to see it. You won't regret it.

This place deserves every penny I paid for it. The building is fascinating, and the surrounding environment is breathtaking. Everyone is enjoying their time here. I highly recommend visiting this place to watch the sunset and wait for the Colosseum to turn orange. I would definitely recommend it after visiting the Colosseum. I’m currently walking around towards the city center.

It's truly a unique experience in Rome, visiting the Colosseum. It is one of the best surviving pieces of ancient architecture and it was the site of the deaths of probably hundreds of thousands of people. Currently the Colosseum is only 40% visible, as 60% was lost to time. It is an incredibly fascinating part of Rome and I would highly recommend visiting it if you are visiting the city, as it is one of the landmark staples that you see and hear about but you don't truly appreciate until you walk through it

A very impressive visit. You can still feel the heaviness of everything that happened here, energetically. The Colosseum was the site of brutal gladiator fights, public executions, and spectacles meant to display power and control. Knowing what unfolded here makes the experience even more intense.

I strongly recommend NOT buying tickets far in advance. Instead, go in person and look for a licensed guide. We paid €50 for the Colosseum, the Roman Forum, and Palatine Hill, compared to €57 on the official website, which also forces you to commit to a time even if you are unsure of your schedule. IF YOU DONT MAKE IT ON TIME, they cancel your ticket (which seems so unfair). Our ticket included a one-hour guided tour, which made a huge difference. The historical context completely changed how we experienced the site.

Tip: bring cash. You will usually get the best price and more flexibility when dealing directly with guides on site.

Visiting the Colosseum was absolutely worth it. It’s massive, impressive, and full of history. Seeing it in person feels very different from photos. Walking around and imagining ancient Rome was really cool. Definitely one of those places you shouldn’t miss when visiting Rome.

Really cool didn’t really know what to expect going in but the lines aren’t bad at all if you purchase tickets beforehand crowds inside and outside are a struggle. The architecture and everything is very nice to see I kind of expected a bit more of it to still be there but it’s still really awesome and impressive how this could stand for nearly 2000 years.

The Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheatre) is the greatest symbol of Ancient Rome and one of the most recognizable architectural monuments in the world. Built in the first century CE, it was an arena for gladiatorial combat, naval battles, and grand spectacles that attracted tens of thousands of spectators.

Officially known as the Flavian Amphitheatre, the name "Colosseum" derives not from the building's size, but from the enormous statue (Colossus) of Emperor Nero that once stood nearby.
Thanks to a system of 80 entrances and exits, 50,000 spectators could leave the building in just 15 minutes.
In the first years after its opening, the arena could be flooded to stage naumachiae—real naval battles.
Throughout history, approximately 400,000 people and over a million animals have died in the arena. For example, the amphitheater's opening celebrations took 100 days, during which time approximately 9,000 animals were slaughtered.
In the Middle Ages, the Colosseum was used as a fortress, stables, and even as a free source of building materials for churches and palaces (including St. Peter's Basilica).

Tip: Colosseum tickets are personalized and in high demand. It's recommended to buy them online several weeks in advance to avoid queues, which can last 1-2 hours.

We had booked a tour guide who even organized the tickets for us in advance, so we didn’t have to wait at all. We were able to enter immediately and explore this imposing masterpiece that has stood here since ancient times. We were absolutely amazed—simply fantastic.

Being inside the Colosseum is a truly powerful experience, especially when you realize that thousands of years ago the Romans held their games right here. It’s overwhelming, impressive, and something you will never forget.

Seeing the Colosseum from the outside is impressive, but stepping inside is on another level. It genuinely feels like you’re walking through history. The way it’s been preserved makes the experience even more powerful and unforgettable

The best experience. I've achieved one more item on my bucket list. There was a lot of confusion due to the large crowd and tourists mixed together, but it was still so good.
Looking at the architecture of Godia Roman, it was amazing that such a building was built 2,000 years ago.
For a moment, I felt like I had become a gladiator of ancient Rome.
Seeing this massive stadium, which accommodates 90,000 out of 50,000 people, I was overcome with thrills.

We had day tickets for 18€ I think, pre-booked a month before going. A fantastic part of our weekend away. We went for the 9am slot worth getting up and being out. 15 minutes before to queue up recommend any earlier and you don't get in. We just walked around no audio was nice and sunny. 2 level access. Not much difference from what we had to the arena floor for a view. Wouldn't waste the extra money for the view. Fantastic part of Rome! Toilets only at the beginning. Gift shop basic. Found better souvenirs else where.

A monumental symbol of ancient Rome and one of the greatest architectural achievements in history. Built nearly 2,000 years ago, the Colosseum once hosted gladiator battles and grand public spectacles for over 50,000 spectators. Standing here, you can truly feel the power, scale, and legacy of the Roman Empire. An unforgettable experience and an absolute must-visit in Rome.

Local Guide tip: Visit early morning or late afternoon to avoid crowds and enjoy better views for photos.

An absolute marvel of engineering and the ultimate symbol of ancient Rome's power. Walking through this massive amphitheater, you can almost feel the echoes of gladiatorial combat and the roar of 50,000 spectators. It is a bucket-list destination that remains just as awe-inspiring today as it was nearly 2,000 years ago.

Can you really go to Rome and not visit The Coloseum? The answer is No. The Coloseum is a must visit.
It really is as magnificent as you would expect, plus there is a whole host of exhibits and information nestled in the stands to also add to the experience.
We opted to include a visit to the arena floor which I recommend as it gives you a real sense of the place. We also used the Y and Co app to help guide us around.
Don't think about booking it, just book it.

GOOD 👍
It's a Wonder Of The World. It will live up to your expectations.

BAD 👎
Errr.... toilet had a slight queue, i guess.

Must see while in Rome.  Really the best part was from outside just taking in the size.  The tour inside is nice but getting great photos from a distance are really nice.   We bought tickets in advance, was a good choice.  No wait.   Bathrooms inside after security.

I’ve visited the Colosseum three times now, and honestly, I wouldn’t go to Rome without seeing it again. It easily ranks among my top travel experiences, alongside the Vatican Museums and St. Peter’s Basilica.

There’s so much history to take in, whether you choose a guided tour or explore on your own. Standing inside such an iconic landmark never gets old, and every visit seems to reveal something new.

I highly recommend booking tickets in advance because the lines can get extremely long without a timed entry reservation. An early morning visit is definitely the best way to maximize your day and avoid some of the crowds. It also gives you plenty of time afterward to continue exploring the surrounding area, including the Roman Forum and nearby ruins.

If you’re visiting Rome for the first time, this is absolutely one of those bucket-list experiences you shouldn’t skip.

Just amazing. Make sure to buy your ticket in advance via the official website. Other websites/vendors charge you way too much.

The queue seems long, but it really goes fast.

Talk about amazing. Flew to Italy for our honeymoon and it was the best thing we ever did. There’s nothing like checking off of your bucket list…. These pictures don’t do justice, you have to see it in real life! Hopefully we’ll return one day with the kids!!! Recommend getting a tour guide!

Visiting the Colosseum was an amazing experience. We did not book a guided tour and instead used ChatGPT as we explored the area, which actually worked very well for learning about the history at our own pace. There is so much history to absorb, and every corner has something interesting to discover.

The site is very easy to reach, as the Colosseo Station takes you directly to the location. A guided tour would also be a great option because it provides direct access to sites and specialist historical knowledge. Honestly, combining a guided tour with using ChatGPT for additional information would probably be the best experience.

As you walk away from the Colosseum, you will also find , ancient arches, temples, and many other historical buildings and ruins. This is where using ChatGPT became especially useful, helping us better understand what we were seeing while exploring independently.

Highly recommended for anyone visiting Rome.

An amazing experience, would highly recommend a guided tour! Not only do you skip the queues they really bring the place to life and make sure you don’t miss anything! The vast scale is impressive, we also toured pallentine hill and the Roman forum with our tour!

As a child, I used to read about the Colosseum and see pictures of it in books and National Geographic, dreaming that one day I would visit it in person. That dream finally came true—and it was absolutely breathtaking. Standing inside such an iconic piece of history felt surreal. We even got to see the underground area where the gladiators lived and the animals were kept before the games. It’s an experience I’ll never forget. Truly a must-see when in Rome!

The Colosseum is one of those places that leaves you speechless — no matter how many times you’ve seen it in photos, standing in front of it is a completely different experience.

Walking through the arches, you can feel the weight of history. It’s not just ruins — it’s a living reminder of centuries of culture, architecture, and stories. The scale is massive, and it’s hard to believe it was built nearly 2,000 years ago.

Tips for travelers:
• Buy tickets in advance online — the lines can get long.
• Guided tours are worth it if you want context and access to areas like the underground or arena floor.
• Bring comfortable shoes; there’s a lot of walking on uneven stone.
• Visit early in the morning or later in the afternoon for fewer crowds and softer light for photos.

Even after exploring inside, it’s worth circling around at sunset — the Colosseum seems to glow as the sky changes colors. And if you want the perfect view for a glass of wine, head across the street to Royal Art Café (where I had dinner with the Colosseum right in front of me).

📍 The Colosseum, Rome — a wonder of the world that truly lives up to its legend. - Michelle

⚔️ Nobody warned me this would change how I see the world.
This is nearly 2,000 years old. Built in just 8 years. No machines. No cranes. 60,000–100,000 slaves transported from Jerusalem constructed the greatest arena in human history.
The numbers are insane:
— 50,000–80,000 people packed inside every event
— 500,000 humans & 1 million animals killed within these walls
— Valued by Deloitte at $90 billion — the most valuable structure on Earth
— The entire crowd could be seated in under 15 minutes. Modern stadiums still copy this design today.
What nobody tells you:
The name doesn't come from its size. It comes from a 35-metre bronze statue of Emperor Nero that once stood outside. Julius Caesar? Never set foot here. He died 15 years before it was built.
🚨 Just opened in 2025:
Emperor Commodus had a secret underground passage hidden for 2,000 years — just opened to the public for the first time. You can literally walk where Roman emperors walked privately to their imperial box. The ancient murals are still on the walls.
The massive gap in the southern wall? An earthquake ripped it apart in 1349. Romans then used these stones to build St. Peter's Basilica.
A place built for bloodshed ended up building one of the holiest sites on Earth. That irony alone is worth the trip.
No photo prepares you. Stand here. 📍

It's definitely one of the must see place. It truly stands it's popularity and what a architectural masterpiece. I would definitely recommend buying tickets in advance or best way to do is via tour guide. You would need a photo ID to enter whether you are going by yourself or through tour guide.
We enjoyed the entire experience and learning about how gladiators used to use this place. Untill I visited the place I never knew it's complexity and vastness.
We travelled as a family with a child. We were told not to take pram as pathways are not smooth, but it's manageable.
I would definitely recommend this place.

This is a must-see attraction when in Rome. The exterior, interior and gardens around it. The history within the walls are difficult to even comprehend. I can’t imagine what some of the ancient warriors felt when seeing this huge structure, full of screaming crowds and knowing they will be the main attraction. Also that it was filled with water for some events. Give yourself several hours or more here.

I thoroughly enjoyed this place and it was definitely worth a visit if you can manage it.  Book your tickets in advance as there’s a lot to do. • Entry & hours: The Colosseum is ticketed (not free); most visitors buy a timed-entry ticket that usually covers the Colosseum, Roman Forum and Palatine Hill. Opening hours vary by season—check the official booking site and buy timed tickets in advance (some ticket types limit stay to ~75 minutes).

• Historic & iconic: Construction began under Emperor Vespasian around AD 70–72 and the arena was formally opened (dedicated) by his son Titus in AD 80; the Colosseum remains the best-known symbol of ancient Rome.

• What to see: Highlights include the surviving outer façade, arena floor viewpoints (on some tours), the underground hypogeum remains, and the neighbouring Roman Forum and Palatine—many visitors combine all three on a single ticket.

• Visitor facilities: Expect audio guides, guided-tour options, temporary exhibitions and basic visitor services; different ticket types grant access to different areas (arena floor or special access tours often sell out).

• Easy to reach: The Colosseo metro station (Line B) and many bus routes stop right by the piazza—it’s extremely central and walkable from Termini and Rome’s historic core.

• Best for photos & timing: Early morning timed entries or late-afternoon light give the best photos and slightly fewer people; guided “after-hours” or restricted-access tours offer quieter views if available.

---

⚠️ What to keep in mind

• Buy official timed tickets: Purchase through the official Colosseum booking site or authorised vendors—there have been major enforcement actions and fines against resellers/brokers who hoard or bot-buy tickets, so avoid third-party scalpers.
• Queues & capacity: Even with timed tickets there can be lines for security checks; arrive with ID and allow time for bag checks.
• Area access varies: Not every ticket includes the arena floor, hypogeum or special levels—check what’s included before you buy.
• Crowds & safety: The site is very busy in peak season—watch for pickpockets in the surrounding piazza and keep belongings secure.
• Conservation & closures: Parts of the monument are periodically closed for conservation or events—check official notices before visiting.

The Colosseum remains the beating heart of Rome, a colossal testament to human ingenuity and the dark complexities of our nature. I have had the privilege of visiting it across all seasons, and its majesty never fades. Whether I am exploring its labyrinthine interior or simply standing in its shadow to admire its imposing exterior during a quick Roman getaway, it never fails to command respect.
It is truly staggering to consider what was built in those ancient times. The Flavian Amphitheatre serves as the architectural blueprint for modern stadiums, yet its history is far more visceral. It was a stage for blood and sand, fueled by the infamous Roman philosophy of "Panem et Circenses" (Bread and Circuses). The emperors knew that as long as the masses were fed and entertained, their power remained unchallenged.
Today, it stands as a silent museum, but its lessons are timeless. While the gladiators are gone, the human psyche remains remarkably unchanged. Even now, if you provide "bread and circuses," you can influence the masses at will. It is a cynical reflection, perhaps, but standing before those arches, you realize that while the stones have weathered, our fundamental instincts have not. It is an architectural marvel and a somber mirror to humanity.

Truly an amazing historical landmark. Standing there is breathtaking and makes you start thinking about the old gladiator days. I can't emphasize enough that buying tickets in advance is a must. What a magnificent and majestic architecture this is. Such a marvel and I wonder how it can be built in the first instance. So amazing and in awe.

The Colosseum is one of those places that evokes feelings of awe and sadness all wrapped into one. The awe comes from stepping foot into such a grand place, walking into a historic monument that once held thousands of cheering patrons. At the same time, you can’t help but feel sad at the thought of the many lives that ended there. Knowing that you’re walking the same path that many walked before you to meet their demise. It’s definitely, a place that deserve both respect for the loss of life and admiration for its grandeur. Definitely worth a visit and time to take everything in.

Colosseum is such an iconic historical landmark that is a must see for visitors to Rome.

Seeing the Colosseum in person is truly unforgettable. Standing in front of this incredible monument, you can feel the weight of almost 2,000 years of history. It’s not just an ancient structure—it feels like a living piece of art that has witnessed centuries of Roman life.

At sunrise, the soft light slowly touches the stone and the Colosseum looks calm and majestic. At sunset, the golden colors of the sky make it even more breathtaking. No matter what time of day you visit, the atmosphere around it is magical.

Walking around it, you can almost imagine the stories, the crowds, and the events that once filled this legendary arena. The architecture, the scale, and the history make it one of the most impressive landmarks in the world.

If you visit Rome, seeing the Colosseum is an absolute must. It’s more than a monument—it’s a masterpiece of history, culture, and timeless beauty.

Despite this, the site itself is incredible, and I highly recommend visiting — just keep your eyes open and stay cautious around the crowds.

We went to the colosseum on the first Sunday of the month when it was free to enter. You can start entering from 8:30am so we got there at 7:30am and the queue was already a good 150 people long. When it opened we only waited 10 minutes to actually go in, but the queue behind us was huge so it is definitely worth getting there early before it opens. The colosseum itself was amazing to witness, all the history and architecture is so impressive. The free entry included access to the forum and Palatine hill too which were also great to experience. Just next to the ticket office is a free water dispenser where you could get still or sparkling water which was great because the temperature was above 30oC. I would definitely recommend to anyone going to Rome and definitely if you're going on the first Sunday of the month, get down early to avoid the big queues.

The basic self-guided tour of the Colosseum and Roman Forum is a fantastic experience, though there are some important logistics to keep in mind. We didn't realize that the two sites have separate entrances: while you must enter the Colosseum strictly at your booked time, the ticket allows you to visit the Forum up to 24 hours before or after, which offers great flexibility. The tour itself is excellent, allowing you to explore two levels and see the interior arena, although I found the English voice on the mobile audio guide a bit difficult to understand. While you get a nice view looking out toward the ruins, the absolute best view of the Colosseum is actually looking back at it from the hill inside the Roman Forum.

A plus to the ticket is that it gives access to Palatine Hill and I would definitely recommend going to it after your visit to the Colosseum.

Beginning in the eighth century B.C., ancient Rome grew from a small town on central Italy’s Tiber River into an empire that at its peak encompassed most of continental Europe, Britain, much of western Asia, northern Africa, and the Mediterranean islands. Nowhere on Earth captures the spectacle of ancient Rome quite like the Colosseum.

Towering above the eastern edge of the Roman Forum, covering more than six acres, the Colosseum remains one of the world’s most famous architectural marvels and is considered one of the Seven Wonders of the Modern World, where my partner, our friends, family member, and I were able to obtain 8:30am entry for self-guided exploration of the first and second levels but not to the arena floor where the gladiators fought. The ticket also includes visits to the Roman Forum, the Imperial Forum, and the Palatine Hill.

Upon arrival, as one walks around the outside, one senses how huge it is, measuring approximately 620 feet in length, 513 feet in width, and over 160 feet in height, with an efficient network of arches, corridors, and staircases to accommodate rapid filling and evacuation for more than 50,000 people.

To enter, one must arrive 15 minutes before the time specified on the ticket. However, it was crowded in mid-September during the week, which would be overwhelming, particularly around peak hours. On the other hand, the line for security checks was short.

It looked very simple on the outside, but it has a very complex interior structure. One can see a lot from the first ring: the center stage/games area; the seating sections reserved for the emperor, the vestal virgins, and the senators; an overview of the now-exposed underground facilities; and the walls of rising seats above you. It can also get crowded here, and one might have to wait for visitors to move on to get a clear viewpoint.

This is a building that captured our imagination since its construction and continues to be this symbol of Rome’s power, of Rome’s brilliance, which we can recommend.

It's by far the most overwhelming place in Rome❤️ Being on the Arena is incomparable with anything else.
I recommend buying tickets in advance, it's very busy, very long lines. We had Full Experience with entry to Arena tickets, bought 7 days before the visit.

Wow...wow...wow this place is so thought provoking. The sheer scale of the place is amazing. You must go & see it. Disabled people & their carer don't pay, but make sure you get your free ticket online first before you visit.

Visiting the Colosseum on Friday, 13 January at 12:00 pm was one of those rare travel moments that genuinely lives up to the hype.

Standing there at midday, with the winter sun cutting through the open arches, the Colosseum feels less like a ruin and more like a living presence. The light at that hour is perfect: warm enough to soften the stone, sharp enough to highlight every scar, crack, and curve left behind by nearly two thousand years of history.

Walking inside is overwhelming in the best way. The sheer scale hits immediately: the towering walls, the layered seating, the arena floor where history unfolded in its rawest form. Knowing that gladiators once stood exactly where you’re standing gives you goosebumps. It’s not hard to imagine the roar of the crowd, the tension in the air, and the drama that once defined this place.

January is an ideal time to visit. The crowds are manageable, which makes the experience more reflective and immersive. You can pause, look up, and truly take it all in without feeling rushed. At noon, the atmosphere is calm yet powerful. Rome doing what it does best: blending everyday life with ancient grandeur effortlessly.

What makes the Colosseum unforgettable isn’t just its architecture or history, it’s the emotion it stirs. It’s humbling, awe-inspiring, and slightly haunting. You leave with a deep respect for the past and a sense that you’ve touched something timeless.

The Flavian Amphitheater? Oh, the Colosseum! One of the most famous and iconic structures of Roman antiquity is the bloody playground we know today as the Colosseum. In 72 AD, Emperor Vespasian commissioned the construction of the largest amphitheater in Roman history. The amphitheater was built on the site of an artificial lake that belonged to Nero's palace, the Domus Aurea; in this way, Vespasian attempted to erase the memory of the hated Nero and regain the favor of the people. The official name is the Amphitheatrum Flavium (derived from Vespasian's family name); the nickname Colosseum is likely derived from the Colossus of Nero, the more than 35-meter-high statue of the reviled emperor, which stood next to the amphitheater, and not from the dimensions of the building, as is often claimed. The building has an elliptical ground plan and measures 188 by 156 meters. The perimeter measures 527 meters, and the 48.5-meter-high outer wall features three rows of 80 arches with half-columns in between. The core of the building is constructed of concrete, tufa, and bricks held together with iron bars. Travertine was used for the façade finish, while marble was applied for the luxury seating, wall cladding, and ornaments. The stands were accessible via 76 public entrances and accommodated 50,000 spectators. The Emperor had a separate box on one of the long sides; on the opposite side were boxes for the Empress, the Vestal Virgins, and the magistrates. The public was protected from the sun by the velarium, an enormous canvas sunshade that could be raised (requiring about a thousand men). The arena itself measures 76 by 44 meters and was covered with yellow sand. Today, the arena floor has almost entirely disappeared, and what is particularly visible is the hypogeum that Emperor Domitian had constructed later (as well as the upper tier of stands). Emperor Vespasian did not live to see the completion of the Colosseum; his son, Emperor Titus, inaugurated the building in 80 AD with games that lasted 100 days. In addition to gladiatorial and wild animal fights, naumachiae (naval battles) are also said to have been re-enacted, but the latter is unlikely. In 404, the Christian Emperor Honorius banned gladiatorial combat; up to that year, an estimated 300,000 to 500,000 people died in the Colosseum. The last animal fight was probably held in 523; how many animals were killed up to that year is unknown. During the 443 years that the amphitheater was in use, damage caused by use and earthquakes was continuously repaired. From the Middle Ages onwards, decay set in: in addition to two severe earthquakes, the reuse of marble, stone, and iron proved particularly fatal to the building. This plundering only came to an end in 1749 when Pope Benedict XIV recognized the historical value of the Colosseum and prohibited its further use as a quarry. Later popes even had the structure (partially) restored, much to the delight of the more than 7 million tourists who visit the C

It’s an absolute must visit in Rome and one of the most iconic landmarks in the world. Built between 72 and 80 AD under the emperors Vespasian and Titus, it is the largest ancient amphitheatre ever constructed, capable of holding up to 50,000–80,000 spectators. This was the stage for gladiator battles, animal hunts, and public spectacles that defined the grandeur of Imperial Rome.

The structure is remarkably well-preserved considering its age, and walking through its arches and underground passages gives you a real sense of its history. Guided tours or audio guides are highly recommended, as they bring the stories of the games and Roman engineering to life.

It does get very crowded, so booking tickets online in advance is essential. A full visit, especially if combined with the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill, can easily take a few hours.

Overall, the Colosseum is Impressive both in scale and history, an unforgettable highlight of Rome.

The Colosseum in Rome is, without question, one of the most magnificent and awe-inspiring monuments in the world. Its sheer scale, historical weight, and architectural brilliance make it a place that leaves a lasting impression the moment you see it. Standing before it, you can’t help but feel connected to centuries of history, imagining the spectacles, the crowds, and the life that once filled its walls. It is truly majestic, a symbol of Rome at its most powerful and enduring.

However, in recent times, it has become increasingly difficult to fully enjoy the experience. The site is overwhelmingly crowded, to the point where simply walking—both outside and inside—can feel frustrating and exhausting. The flow of people is constant and dense, leaving very little space to pause and appreciate the surroundings.

Getting tickets has also become a challenge. Even with the official online booking system, availability is limited, and many tickets seem to end up in the hands of agencies or resellers who take advantage of the demand. This creates an unpleasant sense that the system favors intermediaries rather than individual visitors.

Inside, the situation doesn’t improve much. Large guided tour groups often block pathways and entrances, making movement slow and sometimes nearly impossible. Despite clear rules, some visitors even treat the space casually, stopping to eat or gather in ways that disrupt the experience.

In the end, the Colosseum remains extraordinary—but enjoying it today requires patience, planning, and a willingness to navigate its many challenges.

Colosseum Essentials: How to See Rome’s Icon in a Hurry

The Colosseum is Rome’s most iconic ancient landmark, built between 70–80 AD and preserved today as a UNESCO World Heritage site. During the Roman Empire, this massive arena hosted gladiator battles, animal hunts, and even staged naval battles.
Getting there is simple using the Metro. If you are staying near Termini, take Line B just two stops to the "Colosseo" station. If you are near the Vatican, take Line A to Termini and transfer to Line B.
For the best photos, head to the Palatine Hill overlooking the Roman Forum for a sweeping view of the structure. To go inside and explore its amazing history, be sure to purchase your tickets well in advance, as they sell out quickly.

The architecture wow… it is mind-blowing to think what these walls have seen, hard to describe until you see it in person.
Our guided tours was great add so much insight. In Rome Every corner holds something fascinating.

Strongly recommend booking tickets in advance and including Arena Floor. Can skip Underground experience - it’s really not worth it - you can see it from above and don't feel miss anything by not going down there.

To say we were impressed is an understatement.
It’s hard to believe that almost 2,000 years ago people were able to create such a monumental structure.
The atmosphere inside is incredible. We paid attention to every detail — how the bricks and stones were laid, their size, the staircases, and the thickness of the walls. The feeling inside is truly powerful and unforgettable.
The only thing we regret is that we couldn’t visit the underground levels and the upper level (the higher tier with panoramic views), as these require special tickets.
So if you want the full experience, make sure to book the right tickets in advance.
We paid €18 per person on the official website, which included entry to the Colosseum and the Roman Forum.

I recommend booking your ticket in advance. There are two checkpoints prior to the entrance, which first was to check entry time printed on the ticket and second check point was to scan the QR/barcode and your ID/passport. Awesome visit. We bought our tickets online 2 hours before.. was fast and easy to do

Absolutely breathtaking. I visited on a Wednesday at 9 in the morning and it was a good time to visit. It only cost me 2€ to enter and walk around the 1st and lower floor because I am a EU citizen and under 25 years old

Visiting the Colosseum was an unforgettable experience! The moment you walk up to it, the sheer size and history take your breath away. It’s incredible to think this amphitheater has stood for nearly 2,000 years and was once the center of Roman entertainment.

I recommend booking tickets in advance to skip the long lines, and if possible, join a guided tour — hearing the stories about gladiators and emperors really brings the place to life. Overall, the Colosseum is a must-see in Rome. Standing there feels like stepping back in time, and it’s easily one of the highlights of my trip.

It leaves me in awe - that’s about the best I can say.

hidden gem of rome absolute stunner and the history behind this monument is absolutely breathtaking, the arches around the border of the arena add to the atmosphere of the place and it’s all very beautiful yeah yeah yeah just don’t get scammed outside
An absolute MUST! Amazing place to visit. Oozing with history. Just in awe of the size and scale, incredible. Definitely going back. You will need a full day for the old city!

The Colosseum is just as powerful from the outside as it is within. We chose not to go in certain areas because the crowds, stairs, and uneven surfaces make the interior challenging, but simply viewing it up close was more than enough. Walking (or pausing) around the perimeter lets you appreciate the scale, engineering, and setting without the crush. At the right time of day, especially toward evening, it’s calmer. A reminder that you don’t necessarily have to do everything to truly see Rome.

Must see when in Rome. Book tickets in advance (1 month before your visit date). We didn't manage to get tickets for the full experiences (arena, underground or attic), but even with regular tickets, you get a very good impression and spectacular views.

If you’re going to Rome, the Colosseum is 100% worth visiting. It was my first time there and it really is as impressive as you imagine – incredible inside, amazing photo opportunities, and such a powerful piece of history.
BUT here are some important tips so you don’t make the same mistakes I did:
🚫 1. Book Your Ticket in Advance
I didn’t. They were completely sold out online, but I was determined to go anyway. Big mistake.
📅 2. Avoid the First Sunday of the Month (If You Can)
Apparently, entry is free on the first Sunday of every month. I didn’t know that… which meant it was absolutely packed.
⚠️ 3. Watch Out for Touts
When I arrived, I was approached by loads of people offering “help” and “tours.” I didn’t trust them — until one woman approached me in a queue wearing a badge (which turned out to be fake).
She told me she could get me in on a tour. I paid €30… and all she did was walk me five minutes to another ticket office outside the Colosseum.
👉 If you simply walk to the left, past the Colosseum, there’s another ticket office with a much smaller queue.
Otherwise, the main queue can take about an hour.
I paid by card, thankfully, so I managed to get my money back.
⏳ 4. Expect to Wait
Even after sorting tickets, I waited about two hours to enter. So I made the most of it — took photos, grabbed a drink at a nearby café, then came back.
You can enter 15 minutes before your time slot — just show your ticket and walk straight in.
✨ Inside the Colosseum
Once inside, it was fabulous. Really impressive, fascinating to walk around, and loads of opportunities to get clear photos despite how busy it was. It’s such a massive place that you can still find good angles without crowds in your shots.
⭐ Final Verdict
Yes, it was busy.
Yes, I nearly got scammed.
But was it worth it? Absolutely.
If you’re visiting Rome, you’ve got to see the Colosseum — just book ahead, avoid the first Sunday if you want it quieter, and don’t fall for the “tour helpers” outside.

What a fascinating place! The Colosseum in Rome is one of the most iconic and awe-inspiring historical landmarks in the world. Built nearly 2,000 years ago, this monumental amphitheater once held over 50,000 spectators who gathered to witness gladiator battles, animal hunts, and spectacular shows. Walking through its massive arches and standing where history unfolded offers a powerful connection to ancient Rome’s engineering genius and cultural legacy. Whether admired from the outside or explored inside with tickets that include the arena and underground, visiting the Colosseum is a truly unforgettable experience that brings history to life. A must-see for any trip to Rome! ❤️

An Enduring Masterpiece of Antiquity: The Majesty of the Exterior
​Even if you do not step inside the arena, simply standing on the perimeter and walking around the Colosseum (the Flavian Amphitheatre) is a staggering, must-do experience. The sheer scale of the structure completely dominates the surrounding modern Roman landscape.
​Taking the time to observe the exterior facade is like reading a raw documentary of Roman history. The architecture is a marvel, but what is truly fascinating are the visible scars of time—the exposed brickwork and the missing sections of travertine stone, which were scavenged over the centuries to build other Roman palazzos and churches. It stands not just as a monument to ancient entertainment, but to sheer structural endurance.
​Yes, the surrounding area is heavily crowded with tourists, vendors, and tour groups, which is completely expected for one of the world's most iconic landmarks. However, if you find a good vantage point just slightly elevated from the main walkways, you can truly absorb the magnitude of Roman engineering. A spectacular sight that never loses its power, regardless of how many times you see it.

Built by the Flavian emperors on the drained lake of Nero’s Domus Aurea, the Colosseum was begun under Vespasian around AD 70–72, inaugurated by Titus in AD 80 and completed by Domitian. Its travertine and concrete shell held roughly 50,000–70,000 spectators in tightly segregated tiers, from senators at arena level to women and the poor in the upper rings. The arena, about 83 by 48 metres, had a timber floor over the hypogeum, a two-level system of corridors, cages and lifts for animals, scenery and fighters. Funded largely from war spoils, especially the Jewish War, its games combined hunts, executions and gladiatorial combats in a permanent engine of imperial spectacle.

The visit is well structured, with clear pathways and informative signage. Allowing time to walk slowly and absorb the details makes a significant difference. Despite the crowds, the experience remains powerful and intellectually engaging.

The Colosseum is an absolutely breathtaking place that truly brings history to life. Walking through this iconic structure feels like stepping back in time, imagining the grand spectacles and events that once took place here. The architecture is incredibly impressive, showcasing the ingenuity and craftsmanship of ancient Rome. Every corner of the Colosseum tells a different story, making the experience both educational and emotional.

Despite the large number of visitors, the site is well-organized, and it is easy to navigate thanks to clear signs and pathways. Visiting the Colosseum was one of the highlights of my trip to Rome. It is a must-see destination for anyone interested in history, culture, or architecture. I would highly recommend taking your time to explore and fully appreciate the significance of this incredible landmark.

Visiting the Colosseum was one of those experiences that actually lives up to the reputation. You see photos your whole life, but standing inside it gives you a totally different sense of scale and history. The structure is massive, but what stood out to me most was how detailed and organized everything still is despite being nearly two thousand years old. You can picture exactly how the crowds moved, where the fighters stood, and how the arena functioned.

The audio guide and signage are straightforward and give just enough context without dragging on. I appreciated that you can explore at your own pace and take in the different levels without feeling rushed. It does get busy—there’s no way around that—but it never felt unmanageable.

One thing to note: go early if you want quieter moments. Midday is basically a wall of people. Also, getting the combined ticket with the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill is worth it; all three sites add perspective to each other.

Overall, it’s not just a “check it off the list” landmark. It’s genuinely impressive, historically rich, and absolutely worth seeing even if you’re not usually into ancient sites.

The Colosseum, Rome, Italy

A magnificent ancient amphitheater and one of the New 7 Wonders of the World. It's an absolute must-see landmark.

· The View: It's a truly breathtaking and iconic sight, especially when you see it up close.

· Getting There from Roma Termini:
· Walking: A pleasant 18-minute walk (approx. 1.8 km). A great way to see more of the city.
· Taxi: A quick option, costing around €20.

· Visit Duration: Based on our experience, a full visit to the combined ticket area—which includes the Colosseum, Roman Forum, and Palatine Hill—takes approximately 1.2 hours (1 hour and 12 minutes) to see everything.

· Ticket Tip: It's highly recommended to book your tickets online in advance. This allows you to skip the very long ticket lines and choose a specific entry time.

· Safety: Rome is generally a very safe city.

The Colosseum stands as a remarkable testament to ancient engineering and architectural prowess. Its grand scale and intricate design continue to inspire awe in visitors from around the world. Imagine the historical events that unfolded within its magnificent walls, a true window into the past. Exploring this iconic structure offers a profound connection to centuries of human history. It remains an enduring symbol of a bygone era, captivating all who have the privilege to witness it.

The Colosseum is truly one of the most awe-inspiring historical landmarks in the world. Standing inside this ancient amphitheater, it's easy to imagine the roar of the crowds and the scale of the events that once took place here. The architecture and engineering are nothing short of remarkable, especially considering it was built nearly 2,000 years ago.

That said, be prepared — it gets extremely crowded, even with timed-entry tickets. Lines can be long, and the sheer number of visitors can make it difficult to fully absorb the atmosphere or move around freely.

We booked a guided tour with Crown Tours, and our guide Arturo was outstanding — incredibly knowledgeable, engaging, and genuinely fun. He brought the history to life and made the entire experience so much more enjoyable.

Still, the Colosseum is a must-see when in Rome. Its historical weight and iconic presence far outweigh the downsides of the crowd. Pair it with a walk through the nearby Roman Forum and Palatine Hill for a truly immersive step back in time.

Perfect. Clean, efficient, no visuals — just the plan.

Here’s your 30-minute photography-focused route around the Colosseum, structured for maximum impact and minimum wandering.

⸻

📸 30-Minute Colosseum Photography Plan

⏱ 0–8 Minutes — The Hero Shot

Start at the outer curve of the
Colosseum (Metro side is ideal).

What to do:
• Stand close to the wall.
• Shoot from a low angle upward.
• Focus on repeating arches and stone texture.
• Let the sky occupy the upper third of the frame.

Tip: Slightly underexpose to bring out the depth and contrast in the stone.

This gives you your dramatic opener.

⸻

⏱ 8–15 Minutes — Framed Composition

Walk 2–3 minutes to the
Arch of Constantine.

Best composition:
• Position yourself so the arch frames the Colosseum in the background.
• Step slightly off-center for better depth.
• Zoom slightly (if possible) to compress perspective.

You now have a layered, storytelling image — foreground, monument, sky.

⸻

⏱ 15–22 Minutes — Depth & Scale

Walk along Via dei Fori Imperiali for the overlook of the
Roman Forum.

What to capture:
• A wide shot showing layers of ruins.
• Isolate a single column with the Forum blurred behind.
• Use shadows between structures to create depth.

This gives context to the Colosseum story.

⸻

⏱ 22–30 Minutes — The Signature Close

Return toward the Colosseum outer edge.

Choose your mood:
• Late afternoon: capture warm light hitting the arches.
• Midday: shoot for strong contrast and convert to black & white later.
• Evening: expose slightly darker so the lit arches glow.

Wait a few seconds between crowd movements and shoot when there’s a gap.

⸻

🎯 What You’ll Walk Away With

In 30 minutes you’ll have:
• One dramatic architectural close-up
• One framed composition with depth
• One panoramic historical context shot
• One atmospheric closing image

That’s a complete visual narrative — tight, intentional, and strong.

“Visiting the Colosseum was a truly fantastic experience.
The view was absolutely amazing, with its massive walls and incredible history all around.
Standing there, you can really feel the power and greatness of one of the seven wonders of the world.
An unforgettable moment that I will always remember.

At first glance, the Colosseum doesn’t shout for attention.
It waits.

As you walk closer, the scale slowly sinks in. The stones are worn, uneven, and imperfect, and that’s exactly what makes them powerful. This isn’t a polished monument. It’s a survivor.
Two thousand years later, it’s still standing, still watching.

Inside, the light slips through the arches in a quiet, almost cinematic way. For a moment, the noise fades, and you can imagine the roar of the crowd that once filled this space. It’s strangely calm for a place built for chaos.
You’ll probably find yourself slowing down without realizing it.

My advice: don’t rush for the photos. Take them, of course, but then pause. Look. Breathe. Let the space do the talking.
Many places impress you. This one stays with you.

You can capture the Colosseum on camera, but the feeling of being there, surrounded by history that feels heavy yet alive, is something no photo or video can fully hold.

The Colosseum, Rome: Echoes of an Empire

Visited the Colosseum as soon as I arrived in Rome — and honestly, it was the perfect way to kick off the trip. Even jet‑lagged and straight off the plane, seeing it up close made every photo I’d ever seen feel wildly inadequate.

The scale hits you first. Those colossal arches and stacked tiers still radiate raw power, even after two millennia. I wandered the exterior in awe, trying to picture the roar of 50,000 spectators surging round the arena as gladiators clashed below.

I went back the next day for a walking tour, and it brought the whole place to life — from the blood‑and‑sand drama of the games to the intricate mechanics of the hypogeum tunnels. Seeing it twice really pays off: first for the gut‑punch “wow,” second for the depth and stories.

Despite the crowds, the energy feels reverent rather than chaotic. Everyone knows they’re standing inside something extraordinary — a monument where empire, engineering, and human spectacle once collided.

Tips

Buy skip‑the‑line tickets in advance — absolutely essential. Queues are brutal, especially late morning. Booking ahead saves hours.

Aim for golden hour — the stone blushes gold, shadows stretch long, and the crowds thin just enough.

Combine it with the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill — same ticket, and they complete ancient Rome’s story perfectly.

Good shoes, plenty of water — uneven floors, steep climbs, and blazing sun are all part of the experience.

Verdict

An absolute must‑see and the ultimate Rome essential. Go twice if you can — once to gape, once to understand. It’s living history with heartbeat intact.

I bought a ticket from the official website a month in advance (€18 per adult, 24-hour access) but still couldn’t get a morning entry. I had a combined timed ticket for the Colosseum, Roman Forum, and Palatine Hill with a 12:15 PM Colosseum entry.

Arrive at least 15 minutes early—you’ll still face long lines. Tickets were checked three times, and IDs were occasionally requested, so bring yours. Bags, phones, and umbrellas go through security X-ray.

You must enter the Colosseum at your exact time, and re-entry is not allowed. “24-hour access” does not mean you can enter anytime. It means you can visit all three sites within 24 hours. Palatine Hill and the Roman Forum can be entered anytime during that window, but once you exit each site, you cannot return.

Each site has one restroom. The Colosseum is extremely crowded, so don’t bother using it there. The best option is at the Roman Forum, in the museum building behind the Temple of Venus.

We spent about 4.5 hours total, walking, taking photos, and briefly reading descriptions, while skipping some areas. Visiting Palatine Hill last was a great choice—the afternoon lighting was beautiful.

The Colosseum, originally known as the Flavian Amphitheatre, is the largest ancient amphitheater ever built and remains the most iconic symbol of the Roman Empire's architectural and engineering prowess.
Built between 70 and 80 AD, the Colosseum remains the largest ancient amphitheater ever constructed and a masterpiece of Roman engineering. Originally called the Flavian Amphitheatre, it was funded by the spoils of war and served as a grand gift to the citizens of Rome. The structure could accommodate up to 80,000 spectators who gathered to watch gladiatorial contests, animal hunts, and even mock naval battles. Its sophisticated design featured the hypogeum, an underground network of tunnels and trapdoors used to release fighters and wild beasts into the arena. Despite suffering damage from earthquakes and stone looting over the centuries, it stands today as an iconic symbol of the Roman Empire's power. It is now a UNESCO World Heritage site and continues to be one of the most visited landmarks in the world.
BTW, if you visiting this place, buy ticket in advance. No long queue. Also, DO NOT forget to visit the Colosseum metro station just beside this place. It's beautiful

Finally standing before the Colosseum in real life was absolutely awe-inspiring. This monumental structure exceeds every image, story, and expectation. Its preservation and grandeur let you feel the true genius of Ancient Roman engineering and architecture.

---

⚔️ The Essential Upgrade: The Gladiator Floor

If you want to move beyond just observing and actually step into history, book the Gladiator Floor experience — it’s the best decision you’ll make.

The slight extra cost is 100% worth it. Standing on the reconstructed arena floor gives you chills — it’s a powerful, immersive moment where you can almost hear the roar of the ancient crowd. Because access is limited, the area is peaceful, spacious, and atmospheric, letting you truly absorb the moment and take incredible photos without the crowds.

It transforms your visit from a busy tourist stop into a personal connection with history.

---

🧭 The Upper Floors (Minor Critique)

The general admission upper levels are often overcrowded, making it hard to move or get a clean photo. The energy is still electric, but the space can feel a bit too packed at times — something to expect at one of the world’s most visited landmarks.

Still, the magic of the Colosseum remains undeniable, and the Gladiator Floor access completely makes up for the crowding above.

---

🏟️ Final Thoughts

The Colosseum stands as a testament to ancient brilliance and human endurance.
If you’re visiting Rome, this isn’t just a stop — it’s a journey through time.

💡 Tip: Book your Gladiator Floor tickets in advance to avoid disappointment. It’s the secret to unlocking the most authentic, immersive, and unforgettable Colosseum

The sheer scale and history of the structure are awe-inspiring, and just standing in front of it gives you goosebumps.

It’s a real shame that I only had a weekend to explore Rome, as this iconic landmark deserves much more time. I’m already looking forward to coming back to properly experience it from the inside. Even a short glance at this masterpiece left a lasting impression — truly marvelous.

The Colosseum is one of those places that truly lives up to its reputation. Even if you have seen it in countless photos, the first moment you step out of the metro or turn the corner and see it in person is unforgettable. What makes it even more fascinating is how many small details and stories are hidden behind the massive stone walls. Here are a few fun facts and bits of history that made my visit special:

First, the Colosseum is almost 2,000 years old, having been completed in the year 80 AD under Emperor Titus. At that time it was the largest amphitheater ever built, and it still holds the record today. It could seat around 50,000–70,000 people depending on the event. Imagine a stadium the size of a modern football arena, but built entirely with ancient Roman engineering, using concrete, travertine stone, and an ingenious system of arches.

One thing I learned is that the Colosseum was not only for gladiator fights. Yes, gladiators were the big attraction, but the arena also hosted wild animal hunts, theatrical re-creations of famous battles, and even mock naval battles. For those, the floor could be flooded with water using underground channels, allowing small ships to sail inside. Later, underground chambers called the hypogeum were added, where animals and gladiators would wait before being lifted into the arena with wooden elevators.

Another detail I found surprising is how advanced the seating system was. Roman spectators entered through numbered arches, just like modern stadium gates. Social class determined where you sat: senators and the elite had seats close to the action, while ordinary citizens sat higher up. Women and the poorest Romans were placed in the top rows. The Colosseum also had a retractable awning system, called the velarium, that could be stretched across the top to provide shade. Sailors from the Roman navy were responsible for operating it, which shows how complex it was.

During the Middle Ages, after the Roman Empire collapsed, the Colosseum fell into disuse. It was damaged by earthquakes and even used as a quarry, with its stones recycled into churches and palaces across Rome. That’s why today parts of the outer ring are missing. Despite this, the Colosseum remains one of the best-preserved monuments from ancient times, thanks in part to restoration efforts that began centuries ago.

A quirky piece of trivia is that there is a natural spring near the site, which once provided water for the naval battles. Another lesser-known fact is that the Colosseum has been used as a symbol against capital punishment: since 1999, whenever a death sentence is commuted anywhere in the world, the Colosseum’s night lighting changes from white to golden.

This was my second visit to the Colosseum or more accurately the Flavian Amphitheatre. This time with a group of students. My first visit was solo, when I explored the exterior from every possible angle, taking countless photos and videos of this incredible monument.

The inside of the structure is remarkably impressive and evocative. You can almost imagine what it must have been like in Roman times, when the amphitheatre was filled with spectators from across the empire watching the various spectacles — from gladiatorial battles to exotic animals and even mock naval fights.

If you’re planning a visit, I highly recommend booking a guided tour to avoid the long line-ups. Skipping the line is definitely a smart move. And don’t stop at the Colosseum alone — make sure to add the Roman Forum, located just behind it. The ruins are captivating and worth every extra step.

Stepping up to the Colosseum feels like walking straight into the heartbeat of ancient Rome. No matter how many photos you’ve seen, nothing prepares you for the sheer scale and grandeur of this iconic amphitheater. Its weathered arches and towering stone walls rise with a kind of quiet authority, reminding you that this was once the world’s largest stage—where emperors, gladiators, and roaring crowds shaped history.

Inside, the atmosphere becomes even more powerful. As sunlight spills across the exposed underground chambers, you can almost imagine the energy of the arena at its height: the anticipation, the spectacle, the drama that unfolded here centuries ago. Every corner reveals a story, and every perspective offers a new appreciation for the engineering brilliance of ancient Rome.

What makes the Colosseum extraordinary isn’t just its size, but its soul. It stands as a testament to human ambition, craftsmanship, and the enduring legacy of a civilization that continues to influence the world. Walking its pathways feels both humbling and inspiring—a rare travel moment that stays with you long after you leave.

An absolute must-visit in Rome. If there’s one landmark that captures the essence of the Eternal City, this is it.

Visiting the Louvre was an absolute highlight of my trip to Paris! The sheer scale of the museum is mind-blowing, and the building itself a stunning former royal palace is just as much a masterpiece as the art inside. Looking up at the incredibly ornate, gilded ceilings and walking through the grand galleries felt surreal.

Of course, seeing Leonardo da Vinci’s Mona Lisa in person was an unforgettable moment. Yes, the room is busy, but the line moves quickly and it is completely worth it. Beyond that, the Winged Victory of Samothrace standing tall at the top of the grand staircase and the beautiful Venus de Milo were absolutely breathtaking.

My top tips for anyone visiting:

1. Book your tickets online well in advance to secure your time slot and bypass the massive ticket lines.

2. Wear your most comfortable walking shoes because the museum is massive and you will easily clock in thousands of steps.

3. Pick a few must-see pieces before you go, or you will get easily overwhelmed

My wife and I recently spent our vacation in Paris after traveling from Italy, and we had the opportunity to visit the iconic Louvre Museum during a very hot day, though we were grateful to find plenty of relief in the cool air provided by the shelter of those thick, ancient stone walls. It must be admitted that the vast majority of this museum’s global fame is owed to a single painting by the Italian genius Leonardo da Vinci, which naturally led us to reflect on the sheer superiority and majestic vastness that our home country of Italy, and specifically Florence, continues to offer to tourists from all over the world.

1. The Louvre is the world’s most visited museum, attracting millions of visitors every year.
2. It was originally built as a fortress in the late 12th century before becoming a royal palace.
3. The museum officially opened to the public in 1793 during the French Revolution.
4. The glass pyramid entrance was designed by architect I. M. Pei and opened in 1989. At first, many Parisians hated it. Today it’s one of the symbols of Paris.
5. The Louvre has more than 35,000 artworks on display, but its total collection is far larger.
6. The most famous artwork inside is the Mona Lisa by Leonardo da Vinci.
7. Other must-see masterpieces include the Venus de Milo and the Winged Victory of Samothrace.
8. The museum is so large that if you spent just 30 seconds looking at every object in its collection, it would take months to see everything.
9. During World War II, many artworks were secretly evacuated from the Louvre to protect them from Nazi seizure.
10. The Louvre sits beside the Seine River in the center of Paris and is connected to the historic Tuileries Garden, making the entire area feel like one giant open-air museum.

It's an enormous palace with more art than you can see in a day. I was inaide for 9 hours but still didn't see everything. Highly recommend if you got the time...

The Louvre: Hidden Gems & Fast-Walking Strategy
The Louvre requires a clear strategy. On October 5, 2023, my wife and I explored as "fast walkers," skipping crowded sections to focus on truly irreplaceable works. We tried to enter the museum earlier than our slot but were not successful; stick strictly to your time.
* 3:20 PM [Photos 3 & 4]: Started with a lovely, sweeping coastal view and took a photo of Alexandre-Hyacinthe Dunouy's View of Naples from Capodimonte. Right next to it hung Antonio Joli's The Departure of Charles III from Naples. Seeing all the intricate sailboats leaving the harbor, my instincts immediately told me to get a photo of this!
* 3:29 PM – 3:34 PM [Photo 5]: Made a fast 11-minute transition from the quiet Naples paintings to the Apollo Gallery. A stunning experience of "shining gold." The Crown of Louis XV, with its diamonds and rubies, is a dazzling must-see.
* 3:53 PM – 3:58 PM [Photos 13, 14, & 15]: Arrived at the Mona Lisa at 3:53 PM. My wife had better luck than most people, capturing a pristine shot with no crowds on the frame. Right behind us, the massive The Wedding at Cana was taken showing the full room crowds finishing at 3:58 PM.
* 4:03 PM [Photo 16]: Moving quickly ahead, I found Parmigianino’s famous portrait, Antea. We successfully tracked this masterpiece down by following Ads posted outside the museum.
* 4:08 PM [Photo 17]: Finished with the 18th-century elegance of Thomas Gainsborough's Lady Alston, where I loved the fine details of her blue silk skirt against the trees.
